words.exampleNL: De trein naar Amsterdam vertrekt om negen uur.
words.exampleEN: The train to Amsterdam departs at nine o'clock.
words.audioHint: The 'ei' sounds like 'eye'. Trains are the main form of intercity transport in the Netherlands.

words.exampleNL: De bus stopt hier om de tien minuten.
words.exampleEN: The bus stops here every ten minutes.
words.audioHint: The 'u' sounds like 'u' in 'push'. Same as English.

words.exampleNL: Neem tram 5 naar het Leidseplein.
words.exampleEN: Take tram 5 to Leidseplein.
words.audioHint: The 'a' sounds like 'ah'. Trams are common in Amsterdam, The Hague, and Rotterdam.

words.exampleNL: De metro gaat elke vijf minuten.
words.exampleEN: The metro goes every five minutes.
words.audioHint: The 'e' sounds like 'ay'. Amsterdam and Rotterdam have metro systems.

words.exampleNL: Ik ga met de fiets naar mijn werk.
words.exampleEN: I go to work by bicycle.
words.audioHint: The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'. The Netherlands is the world's cycling capital!

words.exampleNL: Nederlanders fietsen overal naartoe.
words.exampleEN: Dutch people cycle everywhere.
words.audioHint: The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'. Cycling is the most popular form of transport in NL.

words.exampleNL: De auto staat in de garage.
words.exampleEN: The car is in the garage.
words.audioHint: The 'au' sounds like 'ow' in 'cow'. The 'o' at the end is like 'oh'.

words.exampleNL: Vergeet je OV-chipkaart niet!
words.exampleEN: Don't forget your OV-chipkaart!
words.audioHint: OV stands for 'openbaar vervoer' (public transport). Essential card for all public transport in NL.

words.exampleNL: Hoe kom ik bij het station?
words.exampleEN: How do I get to the station?
words.audioHint: The 'tion' is pronounced 'shon'. Stress on the last syllable.

words.exampleNL: De trein vertrekt vanaf perron drie.
words.exampleEN: The train departs from platform three.
words.audioHint: French origin. Stress on the second syllable. The 'r' is slightly rolled.

words.exampleNL: De trein vertrekt van spoor vijf.
words.exampleEN: The train departs from track five.
words.audioHint: The 'oo' sounds like 'oh'. Used interchangeably with 'perron' for platform numbers.

words.exampleNL: De bushalte is om de hoek.
words.exampleEN: The bus stop is around the corner.
words.audioHint: The 'a' sounds like 'ah'. The final 'e' is a schwa. Often combined as 'bushalte' or 'tramhalte'.

words.exampleNL: De trein vertrekt over vijf minuten.
words.exampleEN: The train departs in five minutes.
words.audioHint: The 'v' sounds like 'f'. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: Hoe laat komt de trein aan?
words.exampleEN: What time does the train arrive?
words.audioHint: Separable verb: 'aan' splits off. 'Komt de trein aan?' shows the split.

words.exampleNL: U moet overstappen in Utrecht.
words.exampleEN: You need to transfer in Utrecht.
words.audioHint: Separable verb: 'over' splits off. Very common instruction when traveling by train.

words.exampleNL: De trein heeft tien minuten vertraging.
words.exampleEN: The train has a ten-minute delay.
words.audioHint: The 'v' sounds like 'f'. The 'g' is guttural. Unfortunately a common word for Dutch train travelers!

words.exampleNL: Ik wil een kaartje naar Rotterdam kopen.
words.exampleEN: I want to buy a ticket to Rotterdam.
words.audioHint: Diminutive of 'kaart'. The '-tje' ending is 'tyuh'. Very commonly used.

words.exampleNL: Een enkele reis naar Den Haag, alstublieft.
words.exampleEN: A one-way ticket to The Hague, please.
words.audioHint: The 'ei' in 'reis' sounds like 'eye'. Used when buying train tickets.

words.exampleNL: Een retour Amsterdam, alstublieft.
words.exampleEN: A return ticket to Amsterdam, please.
words.audioHint: French origin. The 'ou' sounds like 'oo'. Stress on the last syllable.

words.exampleNL: De fietsenstalling bij het station is gratis.
words.exampleEN: The bicycle parking at the station is free.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'fietsen' (bikes) + 'stalling' (shelter). The Netherlands has huge bicycle parking facilities.

words.exampleNL: Rij altijd op het fietspad.
words.exampleEN: Always ride on the bicycle path.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'fiets' + 'pad' (path). The Netherlands has excellent cycling infrastructure.

words.exampleNL: Het is vijf minuten lopen naar het station.
words.exampleEN: It's a five-minute walk to the station.
words.audioHint: The 'o' sounds like 'oh'. Also means 'to run' in some contexts.

words.exampleNL: Ik rij elke dag naar mijn werk.
words.exampleEN: I drive to work every day.
words.audioHint: The 'ij' sounds like 'eye'. Can refer to driving a car, riding a bike, or a train running.

words.exampleNL: Heb je al een rijbewijs?
words.exampleEN: Do you already have a driver's license?
words.audioHint: Compound of 'rij' (drive) + 'bewijs' (proof). Both 'ij' sounds are like 'eye'.

words.exampleNL: Ga bij het stoplicht linksaf.
words.exampleEN: Turn left at the traffic light.
words.audioHint: Pronunciation is straightforward. 'Linksaf' means 'turn left'.

words.exampleNL: De supermarkt is aan de rechterkant.
words.exampleEN: The supermarket is on the right side.
words.audioHint: The 'ch' is guttural. 'Rechtsaf' means 'turn right'.

words.exampleNL: Loop rechtdoor tot aan het einde van de straat.
words.exampleEN: Walk straight ahead to the end of the street.
words.audioHint: The 'ch' is guttural. Compound of 'recht' (straight) + 'door' (through).

words.exampleNL: In welke straat woon je?
words.exampleEN: Which street do you live on?
words.audioHint: The 'aa' is a long 'ah'. The 'str' cluster is pronounced clearly.

words.exampleNL: Ken jij de weg naar het centrum?
words.exampleEN: Do you know the way to the city center?
words.audioHint: The 'w' sounds like 'v'. The 'g' at the end is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Ga over de brug en dan linksaf.
words.exampleEN: Go over the bridge and then turn left.
words.audioHint: The 'u' sounds like 'u' in 'put'. The 'g' is guttural. Amsterdam has many bridges.

words.exampleNL: Sla rechtsaf bij het kruispunt.
words.exampleEN: Turn right at the intersection.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'kruis' (cross) + 'punt' (point). The 'ui' sounds like 'ow' with rounded lips.

words.exampleNL: Wacht bij het stoplicht tot het groen is.
words.exampleEN: Wait at the traffic light until it's green.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'stop' + 'licht' (light). The 'ch' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Neem de tweede afslag bij de rotonde.
words.exampleEN: Take the second exit at the roundabout.
words.audioHint: Stress on the second syllable. There are many roundabouts in the Netherlands.

words.exampleNL: De snelweg A2 gaat van Amsterdam naar Maastricht.
words.exampleEN: The A2 highway goes from Amsterdam to Maastricht.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'snel' (fast) + 'weg' (road). The 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Er staat een file op de A4.
words.exampleEN: There's a traffic jam on the A4.
words.audioHint: The 'i' sounds like 'ee'. Very common on Dutch highways, especially during rush hour.

words.exampleNL: De veerboot naar Texel vaart elk uur.
words.exampleEN: The ferry to Texel sails every hour.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'veer' (ferry) + 'boot' (boat). The 'ee' sounds like 'ay'. Ferries connect to the Dutch islands.

words.exampleNL: Het vliegtuig vertrekt van Schiphol.
words.exampleEN: The airplane departs from Schiphol.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'vlieg' (fly) + 'tuig' (craft). Both 'g' sounds are guttural. The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'.

words.exampleNL: Schiphol is het grootste vliegveld van Nederland.
words.exampleEN: Schiphol is the largest airport in the Netherlands.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'vlieg' (fly) + 'veld' (field). Schiphol is below sea level!

words.exampleNL: Zullen we een taxi nemen?
words.exampleEN: Shall we take a taxi?
words.audioHint: Similar to English. The 'a' sounds like 'ah'.

words.exampleNL: We maken een rondvaart met de boot.
words.exampleEN: We're taking a boat tour.
words.audioHint: The 'oo' sounds like 'oh'. Canal boats are iconic in Amsterdam.

words.exampleNL: Amsterdam heeft veel mooie grachten.
words.exampleEN: Amsterdam has many beautiful canals.
words.audioHint: Both 'g' and 'ch' sounds are guttural. Iconic feature of Dutch cities.

words.exampleNL: Loop op de stoep, niet op de weg.
words.exampleEN: Walk on the sidewalk, not on the road.
words.audioHint: The 'oe' sounds like 'oo'. In Dutch, 'stoep' also refers to the front step of a house.

words.exampleNL: Steek over bij het zebrapad.
words.exampleEN: Cross at the crosswalk.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'zebra' + 'pad' (path). The 'e' in 'zebra' sounds like 'ay'.

words.exampleNL: Er is een parkeerplaats achter het gebouw.
words.exampleEN: There's a parking lot behind the building.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'parkeer' (park) + 'plaats' (place). The 'ee' sounds like 'ay'.

words.exampleNL: Hij rijdt op een scooter naar school.
words.exampleEN: He rides a scooter to school.
words.audioHint: Similar to English. Very popular in Dutch cities.

words.exampleNL: De NS heeft een app om kaartjes te kopen.
words.exampleEN: The NS has an app to buy tickets.
words.audioHint: Abbreviation for 'Nederlandse Spoorwegen' (Dutch Railways). The national railway operator.

words.exampleNL: Vergeet niet in te checken met je OV-chipkaart.
words.exampleEN: Don't forget to check in with your OV-chipkaart.
words.audioHint: Separable verb: 'in' splits off. You must check in and out on Dutch public transport.

words.exampleNL: Je moet ook uitchecken als je uitstapt.
words.exampleEN: You must also check out when you get off.
words.audioHint: Separable verb: 'uit' splits off. The 'ui' sounds like 'ow' with rounded lips. Forgetting to check out costs extra!

words.exampleNL: Kijk even op de dienstregeling voor de vertrektijd.
words.exampleEN: Check the timetable for the departure time.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'dienst' (service) + 'regeling' (regulation). The 'ie' sounds like 'ee'.

words.exampleNL: De reistijd is ongeveer een uur.
words.exampleEN: The travel time is about one hour.
words.audioHint: Compound of 'reis' (travel) + 'tijd' (time). Both 'ei' and 'ij' sound like 'eye'.

words.exampleNL: In de spits is de trein heel druk.
words.exampleEN: During rush hour the train is very busy.
words.audioHint: The 'i' sounds like 'i' in 'sit'. Important for planning travel in the Netherlands.

words.exampleNL: Mijn oma rijdt op een elektrische fiets.
words.exampleEN: My grandmother rides an electric bicycle.
words.audioHint: E-bikes are extremely popular in the Netherlands, especially among older people.

words.exampleNL: Heb je een plattegrond van de stad?
words.exampleEN: Do you have a map of the city?
words.audioHint: Compound of 'plat' (flat) + 'grond' (ground). The 'g' is guttural.

words.exampleNL: Wat is uw bestemming?
words.exampleEN: What is your destination?
words.audioHint: The 'e' sounds like 'e' in 'bed'. Stress on the second syllable.

words.exampleNL: Het openbaar vervoer in Nederland is goed.
words.exampleEN: Public transport in the Netherlands is good.
words.audioHint: Often abbreviated to 'OV'. The 'v' in 'vervoer' sounds like 'f'. The 'oe' sounds like 'oo'.
